story: Alaska DNR issued a preliminary decision to convey roughly 20,000 acres (over 31 square miles) of state land near Houston, Alaska to AIDEA, at no charge, for a proposed multi-use industrial and energy development district including data centers. Public comment runs through August 19, 2026.
dedupe_check: FRESH vs full 18-entry ledger (manually reviewed; closest prior entries are 07-02 JBER/Eielson/Clear federal base leases [different agency, different land, different mechanism] and 07-14/07-16 Stak Energy North Slope [different company, different region, different agency])
story_replaces: XPRIZE Wildfire autonomous-drone finals (disqualified: exact repeat of the 2026-07-03 dispatch, missed by dedupe.py's default 7-day check window)

numbers.text: About 19,950 acres, over 31 square miles, of state land
numbers.label: primary, solid not approximate

numbers.text: Conveyed to AIDEA via noncompetitive public/charitable-use conveyance, no price or consideration stated (at no charge)
numbers.source: DNR Online Public Notice id=224431 (primary); corroborated by Dermot Cole + Mat-Su Sentinel
numbers.label: primary + corroborated

numbers.text: Location: about two miles north of Houston, Alaska, in the Mat-Su Borough, east/northeast of the Parks Highway
numbers.source: DNR Online Public Notice id=224431 (primary) + Mat-Su Sentinel + Dermot Cole
numbers.label: primary + corroborated

numbers.text: Public comment deadline: 5:00 PM, August 19, 2026, via DNR
numbers.source: DNR Online Public Notice id=224431 (primary) + Alaska's News Source
numbers.label: primary, live and open as of today

numbers.text: Proposed uses (AIDEA's stated vision, not named in DNR's own notice): data centers, an Alaska Railroad freight/logistics hub, energy/utility corridors, advanced-manufacturing pads
numbers.label: attribute to AIDEA's proposal, not DNR

numbers.text: A trail network used for mushing and recreation overlaps the parcel
numbers.source: Mat-Su Sentinel + Dermot Cole (41.8 miles of trails)
numbers.label: corroborated by 2 sources

numbers.text: City of Houston council ordinance (introduced by Councilmember Lisa Johansen) would ban such development within city limits, citing permafrost-thaw risk and water-supply impact; vote postponed to August 13, 2026
numbers.label: single source, attribute; NOTE the parcel sits about 2 miles outside city limits, so the ordinance would not legally reach it

banned_or_corrected: '$8,300' figure: STRIP, appears in no fetched source
'Mat-Su City Council': WRONG, there is no such body; it is the City of Houston council
'45-page decision signed by John Crowther': single-source (a personal blog), attribute explicitly or drop; DNR's own notice lists contact Daniel Jenkins with no stated page count or signer
'Zero Lake Road' access detail: single-source, minor, attribute or drop
'massive state subsidy' / 'no land valuation published': this is Dermot Cole's opinion characterization (verified as his exact words), attribute explicitly, do not present as neutral DNR fact

quotes.text: There are no pre-determined and set land use decisions made by AIDEA.
quotes.speaker: Randy Ruaro, AIDEA Executive Director

quotes.text: [Decisions would] only be made after consultation with local officials, the Borough, and others.
quotes.speaker: Randy Ruaro, AIDEA Executive Director

fact_check_safe_set.honest_caveat: This is a preliminary, non-binding proposal in the public-comment stage. DNR must still issue a Finding and Final Decision and can modify or reject it. No data-center operators or tenants are confirmed; data centers are AIDEA's stated vision, not even named in DNR's own public notice. AIDEA's own director says no land-use decisions have been made. The opposing Houston ordinance has only been introduced, not passed, and its ban would apply within city limits, while the parcel itself sits about two miles outside those limits, so even a passed ordinance would not legally reach this specific land.
